title: AI Compliance & Risk
description: Learn to navigate the compliance landscape for AI in business — from regulatory requirements to risk mitigation to audit preparation.
learningObjectives: Map the regulatory landscape affecting AI use in your business context and identify which requirements apply to specific AI use cases
Conduct a structured AI risk assessment that identifies, categorizes, and prioritizes risks across data, output quality, and compliance dimensions
Prepare for AI compliance audits by building documentation, controls, and evidence trails that demonstrate responsible AI governance

sections.title: The Regulatory Landscape for AI in Business
sections.content:
AI regulation is evolving rapidly, and businesses need to track requirements at multiple levels — international, national, state, and industry-specific. The landscape is complex, but the core principles are consistent: transparency about AI use, accountability for AI-driven outcomes, and protection of individual rights.

**Major Regulatory Frameworks Affecting AI Use**

Several regulatory frameworks directly impact how businesses can deploy and use AI tools:

• **The EU AI Act.** The world's most comprehensive AI-specific regulation. It classifies AI systems by risk level — unacceptable (banned), high-risk (heavy requirements), limited risk (transparency obligations), and minimal risk (largely unregulated). If your business operates in or serves customers in the EU, you need to understand where your AI use cases fall in this classification. Most business uses of Claude (drafting emails, summarizing documents, analyzing data) fall into the minimal or limited risk categories. But AI used in hiring, credit decisions, or critical infrastructure may be classified as high-risk.
• **GDPR and data protection laws.** Not AI-specific, but they govern how personal data is processed — and AI tools that process personal data are subject to these rules. Key requirements include lawful basis for processing, purpose limitation (data collected for one purpose can't be used for another), data minimization, and individual rights (access, deletion, objection to automated decisions).
• **US regulatory approach.** The US doesn't have a single comprehensive AI law but regulates AI through a patchwork of existing laws and agency guidance. The FTC enforces against deceptive AI practices. The EEOC addresses AI bias in hiring. State laws (like Colorado's AI Act and NYC's automated employment decision law) add local requirements. Industry regulators (SEC, FDA, banking regulators) issue AI-specific guidance for their sectors.
• **Industry self-regulation.** Many industries have developed their own AI guidelines through trade associations or standard-setting bodies. These aren't legally binding in the same way as government regulations, but they represent best practices and often foreshadow future regulatory requirements.

**How to Determine What Applies to You**

The regulatory requirements that affect your AI use depend on four factors:

• **Where you operate.** Companies operating in the EU face the AI Act and GDPR. Companies in California face CCPA/CPRA. Multi-national companies face all of the above simultaneously.
• **What industry you're in.** Financial services, healthcare, education, and insurance have the most stringent AI-related requirements. Technology, marketing, and professional services face fewer industry-specific rules but still need to comply with general data protection and consumer protection laws.
• **What data you process.** AI use cases involving personal data trigger data protection requirements. Use cases involving financial data trigger financial regulations. Use cases involving health data trigger healthcare regulations. The data type, not the AI capability, usually determines the compliance obligations.
• **What decisions the AI influences.** AI that assists humans in making decisions faces lighter regulation than AI that makes autonomous decisions. AI that affects individuals' rights (employment, credit, insurance) faces the heaviest scrutiny. Knowing where on this spectrum your use cases fall helps you prioritize compliance efforts.

**Staying Current**

The regulatory landscape changes frequently. Practical steps to stay informed:

• Subscribe to updates from your legal team or outside counsel. Most law firms publish AI regulation newsletters.
• Follow the regulatory bodies relevant to your industry.
• Schedule quarterly reviews of your AI use case inventory against the current regulatory landscape.
• Participate in industry associations that track and interpret AI regulations for your sector.

sections.title: Risk Identification and Assessment
sections.content:
A structured risk assessment helps you identify what could go wrong with your AI use, how likely each risk is, and how severe the consequences would be. This isn't a one-time exercise — it's an ongoing practice that evolves as your AI use cases mature and the regulatory landscape shifts.

**Categories of AI Risk**

Organize your risk identification around these categories to ensure comprehensive coverage:

• **Data risks.** What could go wrong with the data flowing through your AI systems? Confidential data shared with unauthorized AI tools. Personal data processed without proper consent. Data retained by AI providers in violation of your policies. Inaccurate data leading to flawed AI outputs. Each of these scenarios has different likelihood and impact profiles.
• **Output quality risks.** What happens when the AI produces incorrect, biased, or inappropriate outputs? A factually wrong report sent to a client. A biased analysis that leads to unfair business decisions. An AI-generated communication that offends customers or violates brand guidelines. A hallucinated citation in a document that undermines credibility.
• **Operational risks.** What happens when the AI system fails or becomes unavailable? Over-dependence on AI tools that creates single points of failure. Loss of institutional knowledge as processes become AI-dependent. Model updates from the AI provider that change behavior in ways that break your workflows.
• **Compliance risks.** What regulatory requirements might you violate through AI use? Processing personal data without adequate legal basis. Failing to disclose AI use where required. Making decisions that are subject to fairness requirements using biased AI outputs. Not maintaining required audit trails for AI-assisted decisions.
• **Reputational risks.** How could AI use damage your company's reputation? Public discovery of AI use in contexts where customers expected human judgment. AI-generated content that contains errors, bias, or insensitivity. Data breaches involving AI tools that expose customer information.

**The Risk Assessment Matrix**

For each identified risk, evaluate two dimensions:

• **Likelihood.** How probable is this risk? Rate on a scale from rare (could happen but never has) to almost certain (happens regularly or is expected to happen). Consider both your organization's specific context and industry-wide experience.
• **Impact.** How severe would the consequences be if this risk materialized? Rate from negligible (minor inconvenience, quickly corrected) to critical (significant financial loss, regulatory action, lasting reputational damage). Consider financial impact, regulatory consequences, and reputational effects.

Plot each risk on a likelihood-impact matrix to prioritize your response:

• **High likelihood + high impact = immediate action required.** These risks need controls in place before you proceed with the AI use case.
• **High likelihood + low impact = manage with standard controls.** These risks will happen regularly but won't cause significant harm if handled properly.
• **Low likelihood + high impact = prepare contingency plans.** These risks are rare but devastating when they occur. Have a response plan ready.
• **Low likelihood + low impact = monitor.** Track these risks but don't invest heavily in mitigation.

**Conducting the Assessment**

A practical risk assessment involves these steps:

• **Inventory your AI use cases.** List every way your organization uses AI tools, including informal and personal use.
• **For each use case, walk through each risk category.** Ask: What could go wrong with data? What if the output is wrong? What if the system fails? What regulations apply? How would the public react?
• **Rate each risk.** Use the likelihood-impact matrix. Be honest — underestimating risks doesn't make them go away, it just means you're surprised when they materialize.
• **Prioritize responses.** Focus your mitigation efforts on the risks in the upper-right quadrant of your matrix (high likelihood, high impact).
• **Document everything.** Your risk assessment is a compliance artifact. Regulators and auditors want to see that you identified risks and took reasonable steps to address them.

sections.title: Implementing Controls and Safeguards
sections.content:
Controls are the specific actions, processes, and technical measures you put in place to mitigate identified risks. Good controls are proportional to the risk, practical to implement, and measurable — you should be able to verify that they're working.

**Types of Controls**

Controls fall into three categories, and a robust AI governance program uses all three:

• **Preventive controls** stop risks from materializing in the first place. Examples: restricting which AI tools employees can use (only enterprise-approved tools with proper data agreements), blocking the upload of files classified as confidential, requiring approval before deploying AI in customer-facing workflows, and embedding data classification checks into AI tool interfaces.
• **Detective controls** identify problems after they occur so you can respond quickly. Examples: monitoring AI tool usage logs for anomalies (unusual volume, off-hours access, unexpected data types), sampling AI outputs for quality and bias checks, tracking customer complaints related to AI-generated content, and periodic audits of how teams are using AI tools versus the approved usage policy.
• **Corrective controls** fix problems and prevent recurrence. Examples: incident response procedures for AI-related data breaches, rollback processes for AI-generated content that needs to be retracted, retraining programs when employees violate usage policies, and prompt refinement processes when AI outputs consistently miss quality targets.

**Designing Controls for Common AI Risks**

Here's how to apply controls to the most common AI risks:

• **Data leakage risk.** Preventive: enterprise AI accounts with data protection agreements, data classification training, and technical controls that flag sensitive data before it's sent to AI tools. Detective: regular audits of AI tool usage logs, data loss prevention (DLP) monitoring. Corrective: incident response plan, notification procedures if sensitive data is exposed.
• **Output quality risk.** Preventive: required human review for all externally shared AI outputs, prompt libraries with tested and validated templates, output format validation for structured outputs. Detective: random sampling of AI outputs for quality review, tracking error rates and revision cycles. Corrective: prompt refinement process, feedback loops from reviewers, escalation procedures for systematic quality issues.
• **Compliance risk.** Preventive: compliance review of new AI use cases before deployment, data processing agreements with AI providers, legal review of AI use in regulated activities. Detective: compliance monitoring dashboards, regulatory change tracking. Corrective: remediation plans for compliance gaps, regulatory notification procedures where required.

**Making Controls Practical**

Controls that are too burdensome don't get followed. Design controls with usability in mind:

• **Embed controls into workflows** rather than adding separate steps. A data classification prompt that appears when opening the AI tool is more effective than a quarterly training session about data classification.
• **Automate where possible.** Automated DLP scanning, format validation, and usage logging reduce the human effort required to maintain controls.
• **Be proportional.** High-risk use cases (AI in hiring decisions) warrant heavyweight controls (legal review, bias testing, documented justification). Low-risk use cases (AI for meeting note summaries) need lightweight controls (quick human review before sharing).
• **Measure effectiveness.** For each control, define how you'll know it's working. Track the number of incidents caught by detective controls, the compliance rate with preventive controls, and the time to resolve issues addressed by corrective controls.

sections.title: Audit Preparation and Documentation
sections.content:
Whether it's an internal audit, a client due diligence review, or a regulatory examination, being able to demonstrate that you've thought about AI risks and implemented appropriate controls is increasingly important. Good documentation is the foundation of audit readiness.

**What Auditors and Reviewers Look For**

Auditors assessing AI governance typically evaluate four areas:

• **Awareness and intent.** Has the organization recognized that AI use creates risks? Is there a governance framework that addresses these risks? Auditors want to see that you've thought about AI governance intentionally, not that you've just been using AI tools without any consideration of the implications.
• **Policy and process.** Are there documented policies governing AI use? Do these policies cover the key risk areas (data handling, output quality, compliance, accountability)? Are the policies communicated to and understood by employees? A policy that exists but isn't followed is worse than no policy — it suggests you know the risks but aren't managing them.
• **Controls and monitoring.** What controls are in place to mitigate identified risks? Are these controls operating effectively? Is there evidence that they're working — monitoring logs, review records, incident reports? Auditors don't just want to see controls on paper; they want evidence that the controls are functioning.
• **Continuous improvement.** How does the organization learn from AI-related incidents and near-misses? Is there a process for updating policies and controls as the AI landscape evolves? Auditors view continuous improvement as a sign of mature governance.

**Essential Documentation to Maintain**

Build and maintain these documents to ensure you're always audit-ready:

• **AI use case inventory.** A comprehensive list of how AI tools are used across the organization, including the tools used, data involved, outputs produced, and individuals responsible. Update this quarterly or whenever new use cases are deployed.
• **Risk assessment records.** Your risk assessments, including the identified risks, their ratings, and the controls you've implemented to address them. Keep historical versions so you can show how your risk thinking has evolved.
• **Policy documents.** Your AI usage policy, data handling guidelines, review requirements, and any supplementary procedures. Include version history showing when policies were updated and why.
• **Control evidence.** Logs, reports, and records that demonstrate your controls are operating. Usage logs from AI tools, output review records, training completion records, incident reports and resolution documentation. This is the evidence that your governance framework is more than just paper.
• **Decision documentation.** For significant decisions about AI use — deploying a new use case, selecting an AI provider, accepting a risk, modifying a control — document the decision, the reasoning, and who approved it. This shows auditors that decisions were made deliberately by accountable individuals.

**Building an Audit Trail**

An audit trail connects the dots between your policies, controls, and evidence:

• **Log AI usage systematically.** Use enterprise tools that capture who used which AI tool, when, and for what purpose. Many enterprise AI platforms provide usage analytics — review them monthly.
• **Document review and approval.** When AI outputs are reviewed before use (especially for medium and high-risk use cases), record who reviewed the output, when, and what the outcome was (approved, approved with edits, rejected).
• **Record incidents and responses.** When something goes wrong — a data handling mistake, a quality failure, a compliance concern — document what happened, how it was discovered, what impact it had, how it was resolved, and what changes were made to prevent recurrence.
• **Track policy compliance.** Periodically assess whether teams are following the AI usage policy. Document the assessment results and any corrective actions taken. This proactive monitoring demonstrates to auditors that you don't just have policies — you enforce them.

**Preparing for Specific Audit Types**

• **Internal audits.** Usually focused on whether AI governance policies are being followed and whether controls are effective. Prepare by reviewing your control evidence and ensuring documentation is current.
• **Client due diligence.** Clients increasingly ask about AI governance as part of vendor assessments. Prepare a summary document that explains your AI governance framework, key policies, and controls without exposing proprietary details.
• **Regulatory examinations.** Focus on compliance with specific regulations. Prepare by mapping your AI use cases to applicable regulations and assembling evidence of compliance for each. Have your legal team review your documentation before the examination.

sections.title: Perform an AI Compliance Review
sections.content: Practice conducting a structured compliance review for a realistic business AI use case.

exercise.prompt:
A mid-sized insurance company wants to use Claude to help claims adjusters process incoming claims faster. Here's the proposed workflow:

1. Customer submits a claim with supporting documents (photos, police reports, medical records)
2. Claude reads the claim and supporting documents, extracts key information (claim type, damage amount, policy details), and generates a structured summary
3. Claude flags any inconsistencies or potential fraud indicators based on the extracted information
4. A claims adjuster reviews Claude's summary and flags, then makes the final decision on the claim

Conduct a compliance review of this proposed AI use case:

1. **Regulatory Mapping** — Identify at least 3 specific regulations or regulatory requirements that apply to this use case. For each, explain what obligation it creates.
2. **Risk Assessment** — Identify the 3 highest-priority risks (use the likelihood-impact framework). Rate each as high/medium/low on both dimensions and explain your rating.
3. **Controls Recommendation** — For each of the 3 risks you identified, recommend one preventive control and one detective control. Be specific about what the control does and how it operates.
4. **Documentation Requirements** — List the 5 most important documents or records this company should maintain for audit readiness related to this AI use case.
5. **Go/No-Go Recommendation** — Based on your review, should the company proceed with this use case? Under what conditions?

Be specific to the insurance claims context — generic AI governance advice won't be useful here.
hints: Insurance claims involve sensitive personal data (medical records, financial information), which triggers specific data protection requirements
The fraud detection aspect raises fairness and bias concerns — if Claude's flags are systematically biased against certain groups, that creates regulatory and legal liability
Consider whether this use case would be classified as 'high risk' under the EU AI Act — insurance claims processing is specifically mentioned in several regulatory frameworks
The human-in-the-loop design is a strength from a compliance perspective, but only if the human review is genuine and documented — not just rubber-stamping Claude's recommendations
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) At least 3 specific, correctly identified regulations — likely including insurance regulations (state insurance department requirements), data privacy laws (GDPR if applicable, state privacy laws), and potentially the EU AI Act's high-risk classification for insurance. Each regulation should have a specific obligation tied to this use case, not just a general description of the law. (2) Three risks with likelihood and impact ratings that are justified — risks should be specific to insurance claims processing (not generic AI risks) and should cover data handling (medical records), output quality (incorrect fraud flags), and compliance (regulatory classification of AI in insurance). (3) Specific, implementable controls — not just 'review outputs' but detailed controls like 'all medical records must be processed through a HIPAA-compliant AI instance with a BAA in place' (preventive) and 'monthly bias audit of fraud flags by demographics' (detective). (4) Five documentation requirements that are relevant to insurance claims AI — claim processing logs, adjuster review records, bias monitoring reports, data processing agreements, and risk assessment documentation. (5) A go/no-go recommendation with conditions that demonstrate understanding of the compliance requirements — likely a conditional go with specific compliance prerequisites. Deduct points for generic risk assessments not specific to insurance, missing the healthcare data angle (medical records), or controls that don't address the specific risks identified.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
AI compliance and risk management is about systematic thinking, proportional responses, and thorough documentation:

• **Know your regulatory landscape.** The regulations that apply to your AI use depend on where you operate, what industry you're in, what data you process, and what decisions the AI influences. Map your use cases to applicable requirements before you deploy.

• **Assess risks systematically.** Use the likelihood-impact matrix to prioritize your attention. High-likelihood, high-impact risks need immediate controls. Low-likelihood, low-impact risks need monitoring. Don't treat all risks equally — focus your resources where they matter most.

• **Implement layered controls.** Preventive controls stop problems. Detective controls catch problems. Corrective controls fix problems. A mature AI governance program uses all three types, with the intensity of controls proportional to the risk level of each use case.

• **Documentation is your best defense.** In an audit, what you can prove matters more than what you've done. Maintain comprehensive records of your governance framework, risk assessments, controls evidence, and decision documentation. If it's not documented, it didn't happen.

• **Human-in-the-loop is a compliance asset — if it's genuine.** Having a human review AI outputs before they take effect is one of the strongest compliance arguments you can make. But the review must be real — documented, by a qualified person, with the authority to override the AI. A rubber stamp adds risk, not protection.
